reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong decision-support framing throughout — every major choice (Lambda vs container vs batch, ALB vs API Gateway, Aurora vs RDS) includes concrete when-to-use and when-not-to-use guidance, not just configuration recipes.

Pricing figures grounded in the source files are correctly cited: ALB 'Flat ~$18/month + usage' and API Gateway 'Pay-per-request (~$1/million requests)' match the JSDoc verbatim from web-services.d.ts.

Code examples use the correct <CodeBlock intellisense> pattern with defineConfig and class-based resources throughout. The batch job retryConfig structure (attempts, retryIntervalSeconds, retryIntervalMultiplier) matches batch-jobs.d.ts exactly.
FeatureComparisonTable for compute models is a well-structured decision aid — scale-to-zero, pricing model, max run time, and ARM support in a single scannable view.

FAQ section covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (custom domains, env vars), AWS service use-case (DynamoDB on-demand vs provisioned, Fargate scale-to-zero), and comparison (web service vs Lambda, hosting options).
The opening <Info> callout correctly orients readers to related Console features (cost dashboards, per-resource breakdown, budgets) before diving into optimization — good information architecture.
The cost optimization checklist at the end is a practical artifact readers can keep open while auditing a stack, not just filler text.

The ARM/Graviton section explicitly covers Lambda, containers, and Redis clusters — three different resource types with one coherent recommendation, showing good union-type coverage across resource domains.
problems: Several specific cost figures not present in the provided source files are stated as facts without qualification: NAT Gateway ~$32/month, CDN ~$0.01/10,000 requests + per-GB, CDN hosting-bucket ~$0.01/10,000 requests, AWS budget cost ~$0.02/day. These may be accurate AWS list prices but cannot be verified from the supplied source files.

The log retention defaults table (Lambda: 180 days, containers: 90 days, batch jobs: 90 days, relational databases: 90 days) is presented authoritatively but only the batch-jobs.d.ts 90-day default is visible in the provided source. If any other default is wrong, the table misleads readers who plan retention budgets based on it.

'Reduce the default `timeout` from 10 seconds' asserts a specific default for Lambda that is not confirmed in the provided source files. If the actual default differs, this guidance is incorrect and a first-time user following it literally could create over-restrictive functions.

Terms 'ACU', 'provisioned concurrency', and instance family letters (t, r, m, c, g) are used without enough context for a reader with low AWS experience. The target persona may not know what 't4g' or 'r7g' means, or why '~1 ACU provides ~2 GB RAM' is actionable information.

The 'Cost optimization checklist' section restates every prior H2 in checkbox form without adding new information. While useful as a quick-reference artifact, it risks reading as padding — especially since the prior sections already end with opinionated Tip callouts summarizing the same recommendations.
optionalImprovements: For each unverified cost figure (NAT Gateway, CDN pricing, budget cost), either confirm from a source file or add a 'check current AWS pricing' link so readers know the figure may change. Alternatively, use ranges or say 'roughly' and link to the AWS pricing page. Do not invent or harden numbers that aren't in source.
Verify the Lambda default timeout against the lambda-function types source file. If it is not 10 seconds, correct the sentence. If it is 10 seconds, add a brief note confirming where to see it.

Add a one-sentence plain-English gloss for ACU ('Aurora Capacity Unit — roughly 2 GB RAM and proportional CPU'), instance family letters ('t = burstable, good for workloads with occasional spikes; r = memory-optimized for sustained high-memory workloads'), and provisioned concurrency ('keeps instances warm between requests at a continuous cost') so readers with low AWS experience can act on these recommendations without a side-trip to AWS docs.

Merge or convert the checklist section into a collapsible aside or drop-down note rather than a full H2 section. A checklist is high-value as a sidebar or callout but feels redundant as a peer H2 alongside the twelve detailed sections above it.

The batch job ARM/Graviton entry in the FeatureComparisonTable is 'false'. Consider adding a brief footnote or parenthetical explaining why — the batch-jobs.d.ts source shows no architecture property and the instanceTypes override is commented out — so readers understand this is a current Stacktape API limitation, not an AWS limitation.

The 'Use dev mode during development' section is the most loosely connected to cost — it reduces iteration cost and time but not AWS billing in the typical sense. Either open the section with an explicit framing ('dev mode avoids deploying real AWS resources during development, which saves on idle container and database time') or fold it earlier as a sub-point under compute model selection.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Strong opening paragraph: clearly separates AWS billing from Stacktape subscription fees and frames the page's purpose without preamble.
Load balancing pricing (~$1/million for http-api-gateway, ~$18/month for ALB) is directly grounded in the web-services.d.ts JSDoc — the most important pricing comparison on the page is sourced.

BatchJob spot instances and retry config example matches the source exactly: useSpotInstances, retryConfig.attempts, retryConfig.retryIntervalSeconds, retryConfig.retryIntervalMultiplier are all confirmed in batch-jobs.d.ts.

The FeatureComparisonTable for compute models (Lambda / container service / batch job) is a standout element — covers pricing model, scale-to-zero, max run time, and traffic pattern suitability in one scannable view.
Cost optimization checklist at the end is directly actionable: a production engineer can run through it against any stack in under five minutes.
FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service behavior, choosing/comparison) with substantive 2-4 sentence answers.

No forbidden format patterns detected: all Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs=[...]>, no hand-authored YAML, no plain-object StacktapeConfig, no fenced TypeScript blocks with defineConfig.
Spot instances section explicitly names the failure modes (non-idempotent side effects, strict deadlines) that make spot unsafe — exactly the kind of caveat a production engineer needs.
BatchJob retentionDays default of 90 days in the log retention table is confirmed by the @default annotation in BatchJobLogging.
problems: UNSOURCED STACKTAPE BEHAVIORAL CLAIM: 'Lambda functions with joinDefaultVpc: true lose direct internet access' is a specific Stacktape behavioral statement not present in any provided source file. A production engineer configuring Lambda VPC connectivity would act on this.

UNSOURCED STACKTAPE BEHAVIORAL CLAIM: 'Stacktape auto-creates VPC endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB' is a specific Stacktape behavior claim not present in any provided source file. If wrong, it would cause silent production failures.

Lambda default log retention listed as 180 days in the retention table cannot be verified from provided source files. The only retention default visible in source is BatchJobLogging @default 90. If the Lambda default is 30 or 365 days, the table would mislead engineers deciding how much to cut.

Multiple specific AWS pricing figures in main content body are not present in the provided source files: NAT Gateway ~$32/month; Redis cluster ~$0.016/hr (~$12/month); private service service-connect ~$0.50/month; CDN ~$0.01/10k requests and ~$0.085/GB; RDS t4g.micro ~$12/month and t4g.medium ~$50/month; budget alerts ~$0.02/day. AWS list prices drift frequently and vary by region — stating them without qualification or source risks the engineer over/under-estimating tradeoffs.

ARM architecture for container services ('set architecture: arm64 on the resources property') is stated but the resources subtype for WebService is not expanded in web-services.d.ts — SimpleServiceContainer and its fields are not provided. The property path cannot be confirmed from provided source.
mandatoryFixes: Verify or remove the two Stacktape-specific Lambda VPC behavioral claims from the 'Avoid unnecessary NAT Gateways' section: (1) 'Lambda functions with joinDefaultVpc: true lose direct internet access' and (2) 'Stacktape auto-creates VPC endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB.' These are high-severity because a production engineer will configure VPC based on them. Either ground them in the Lambda function source type (types/stacktape-config/lambda-functions.d.ts) or replace with a link to the Lambda networking docs and remove the specific behavior claims.

Correct or remove the Lambda log retention default (180 days) in the retention table. The only retention @default visible in provided source is BatchJobLogging at 90 days. If Lambda's source type shows a different default, the table is wrong. Either verify against types/stacktape-config/lambda-functions.d.ts or replace the Lambda row default with a verified value or a dash with a source link.

Qualify or remove specific AWS pricing figures not present in provided source files (NAT Gateway ~$32/month, Redis cluster ~$0.016/hr, service-connect ~$0.50/month, CDN request/transfer rates, RDS t4g.micro/medium monthly costs, budget ~$0.02/day). Per the pricing rules, only prices from source files may be stated as fact. For grounded prices (ALB ~$18/month, API Gateway ~$1/million from web-services.d.ts JSDoc), keep them. For all others, either ground them by reading the relevant price source or replace with directional language ('Redis clusters bill per hour; check current pricing in the AWS console') and remove the specific dollar figures.
optionalImprovements: The 'Use dev mode during development' section is tangential for a cost optimization page targeted at a production engineer reviewing a live stack. Consider trimming it to two sentences or moving it to a 'development workflow' costs note within an existing section rather than a standalone H2.

The Fargate CPU/memory table is useful but its numbers are not in the provided source files. Adding a one-line note ('as defined by AWS Fargate task size limits') and a link to the web-service or container docs page would give the engineer a path to verify if AWS updates the table.

The ARM/Graviton recommendation for Redis ('cache.t4g.micro, cache.m7g.large') could link to the Redis cluster resource page rather than listing instance names inline — instance families are likely to change and the cluster page is authoritative.

FAQ answer for 'How do I reduce Lambda cold start costs without provisioned concurrency?' claims 'arm64 (Graviton processors start faster)' — this is a reasonable general claim but worth softening to 'can reduce initialization time for some runtimes' since the speedup varies significantly by runtime and package size.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, within the target length, and clearly distinguishes AWS billing from Stacktape subscription cost.
The page is highly scannable: short H2s, practical subsections, comparison table, checklist, and a broad FAQ with Stacktape, AWS-service, and choosing/comparison questions.
Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/> with TypeScript tabs and class-based resources, avoiding the forbidden YAML and StacktapeConfig styles.

The load-balancing and batch-job Spot guidance mostly aligns with the provided web-service and batch-job source: API Gateway default pricing, ALB base cost, ALB feature requirements, NLB protocol use, Spot interruption, SIGTERM timing, and retryConfig are all source-backed.
problems: Major source-grounding failure: most of the page depends on facts not present in the provided source files, including Lambda architecture/pricing/memory defaults, Fargate resource combinations, database engine pricing and behavior, DynamoDB defaults, Upstash Redis, CDN pricing, NAT Gateway behavior, dev mode behavior, budget pricing, Console metrics flows, and log retention defaults for resources other than batch jobs.

The page states unsupported or likely overbroad claims as facts, such as `arm64` saving ~20% across Lambda/Fargate/Redis, Fargate minimum 1 running instance, Lambda cold starts of 1-5 seconds, API Gateway crossover at ~500k requests/day outside the load-balancing section, and `Batch job` ARM/Graviton support as false in the comparison table.

The ARM/Graviton section introduces properties and behavior not visible in the provided types. The source files shown do not expose `architecture` on Lambda, container resources, Redis clusters, or BatchJob, so the section cannot be verified from the given sources.

The right-sizing and database sections contain many concrete numeric values not grounded in the provided sources: Lambda vCPU memory thresholds, timeout default, Fargate CPU/memory matrix, Aurora Serverless v2 ACU range, RDS monthly costs, Redis hourly cost, CloudFront request/GB pricing, NAT Gateway monthly cost, and AWS Budget pricing.

The log retention section is partially wrong relative to the provided sources: BatchJob logging retention default is grounded as 90 days, but Lambda, container workload, and relational database defaults are not present in the provided source files.

The page describes Stacktape Console cost dashboards, per-resource breakdown, metrics views, and budget alert scoping without console-app sources. The prompt explicitly warns not to invent Console workflows or UI behavior when console-app source is not included.

Several opt-in sections are helpful but not source-grounded enough to publish: CDN, NAT Gateway, dev mode, budget alerts, ARM, and database engine selection need either supporting source files or narrower wording.
The FAQ repeats unsupported technical claims from the body, especially around Fargate scale-to-zero, Graviton savings, DynamoDB defaults, CDN pricing, static hosting costs, and budget behavior.
mandatoryFixes: Ground or remove every Stacktape-specific technical claim that is not supported by the provided web-service and batch-job source files. Keep the web-service load-balancing and batch-job Spot sections; narrow or delete unsupported sections unless the relevant source files are added.
Remove or source the ARM/Graviton section. The provided source files do not show an `architecture` property for Lambda functions, container workloads, Redis clusters, or batch jobs.
Remove or source Lambda-specific pricing, cold-start, memory, timeout, provisioned concurrency, and reserved concurrency claims. None of those Lambda facts are supported by the provided source files.

Remove or source the Fargate CPU/memory matrix, scale-to-zero claim, autoscaling example, and container right-sizing guidance. The provided web-service source does not include the `resources` or `scaling` property definitions.

Replace the log-retention table with only the source-backed BatchJob default of 90 days, or add the source files for Lambda, container workloads, and relational databases before documenting their defaults.
Remove or source all database, DynamoDB, Redis, Upstash Redis, CDN pricing, NAT Gateway, dev mode, budget alert, and static hosting claims. These are not grounded in the provided source files.

Remove Console UI and dashboard behavior claims unless console-app sources are provided. You may link to cost pages cautiously, but do not describe Console capabilities or workflows without source support.

Correct the batch-job comparison table or remove unsupported cells. The provided BatchJob source supports pay-only-for-compute-time, retries, CPU/GPU workloads, triggers, and Spot interruption, but it does not support `ARM/Graviton: false` or unlimited runtime.

If keeping cost guidance for web-service load balancing, scope it precisely to the source: `http-api-gateway` is the default and costs about $1/million requests; `application-load-balancer` has about $18/month base cost plus usage and is more cost-effective above about 500k requests/day; `network-load-balancer` is for TCP/TLS and requires explicit ports.
optionalImprovements: Consider splitting this into a narrower source-grounded page focused on web-service load balancing and batch-job Spot savings, then expand after adding source files for Lambda, container workloads, databases, observability, Console costs, and budgets.
Add a short table for the three web-service load-balancing variants using only the source-backed feature and cost distinctions.
For batch jobs, add the source-backed retry defaults: `attempts` defaults to 1, `retryIntervalSeconds` defaults to 0, and `retryIntervalMultiplier` defaults to 1.
Keep the checklist only after pruning unsupported items; otherwise it creates a high-density block of ungrounded recommendations.

verifierResults.summary: The draft contains several high-impact Stacktape-specific claims that are not grounded in the provided source files, especially around web service resource shape and batch job capabilities. The strongest issues are incorrect property paths and unsupported resource comparisons that would affect how readers configure resources.

issues.statement: "For container-based resources ... set `architecture: 'arm64'` on the `resources` property. This applies to Fargate workloads" and the web service example sets `resources: { cpu: 0.5, memory: 1024, architecture: 'arm64' }`.
issues.reason: The provided WebServiceProps source does not define a top-level `resources` property or an `architecture` field. WebServiceProps extends `SimpleServiceContainer`, but that type is not provided here, so this draft cannot ground the claimed property path from the supplied source. This would directly affect the user's Stacktape config.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove or narrow the web service `resources.architecture` guidance unless the relevant `SimpleServiceContainer` source is provided and confirms the property path. If keeping ARM guidance, ground it only in source files that expose the exact property.

issues.statement: The compute comparison table says batch jobs do not support ARM/Graviton: `ARM/Graviton ... Batch job: false`.
issues.reason: The provided BatchJob source does not document an `architecture` setting or any ARM/Graviton support status. A false support claim changes what readers choose for batch workloads and is not grounded in the supplied source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the batch-job ARM/Graviton row or phrase cautiously: "The provided BatchJob source does not expose an architecture setting."

issues.statement: "Batch jobs support `useSpotInstances`, which runs your job on spare AWS capacity at up to 90% discount."
issues.reason: The source supports the `useSpotInstances` property and the "saves up to 90%" claim, but it does not say Stacktape runs the job on "spare AWS capacity." That is AWS background context, but the sentence presents it as Stacktape-specific behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the source wording: "Batch jobs support `useSpotInstances`, which uses discounted capacity and can save up to 90%, but jobs can be interrupted."

evidence.quote: #### Use discounted spare AWS capacity. Saves up to 90%, but jobs can be interrupted.

issues.statement: The "Use spot instances for batch jobs" example sets `retryConfig: { attempts: 3, retryIntervalSeconds: 30, retryIntervalMultiplier: 2 }` without explaining that the source default is `attempts: 1`, `retryIntervalSeconds: 0`, and `retryIntervalMultiplier: 1`.
issues.reason: The example uses specific tunable retry values but does not anchor what the values control or how they differ from defaults. Readers may copy the values without understanding the retry behavior they are changing.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short sentence after the example explaining that `attempts` caps retry count, `retryIntervalSeconds` sets the initial wait, and `retryIntervalMultiplier` creates exponential backoff; mention the documented defaults.

evidence.quote:
#### Max retry attempts before the job is marked as failed.
   * @default 1
#### Multiply wait time by this factor after each retry (exponential backoff).

issues.statement: "For private services, the default `service-connect` costs ~$0.50/month — far cheaper than the `application-load-balancer` option at ~$18/month."
issues.reason: The provided sources include WebService and BatchJob only. They do not include PrivateService source or any `service-connect` property/default. The ALB price is present for web services, but the private service default and price are not grounded in the supplied source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the private-service `service-connect` pricing/default claim unless the private service type source is included. Keep the web service load-balancing comparison grounded in `WebServiceProps`.

evidence.quote:
- **`application-load-balancer`**: Flat ~$18/month + usage. Required for gradual deployments
   *   (`deployment`), top-level firewalls (`useFirewall`), and WebSocket support.

issues.statement: "Fargate bills for the exact CPU/memory combination you request" and the table of valid Fargate CPU/memory combinations.
issues.reason: The provided WebService source does not expose the container resource shape or valid CPU/memory combinations. These may be AWS facts, but the draft presents them in the context of Stacktape configuration and includes a Stacktape WebService example using `resources`, which is not grounded in the supplied source.
issues.suggestedFix: Either provide the `SimpleServiceContainer` source that defines `resources`, or remove the Stacktape-specific CPU/memory combination table and example from this page revision.

issues.statement: The page makes many resource-specific cost claims for Lambda, Redis, relational databases, DynamoDB, Upstash Redis, CDN, NAT Gateway, dev mode, and budgets.
issues.reason: Those topics are outside the two provided source files. Some may be valid AWS background or grounded in other Stacktape source files, but they cannot be verified from the supplied WebService and BatchJob sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Provide the relevant source files for the resources covered, or narrow the page to web service and batch job cost optimization facts supported by the supplied sources.

positiveFindings: The web service load balancing section correctly names all three variants present in `WebServiceProps`: `http-api-gateway`, `application-load-balancer`, and `network-load-balancer`.
The draft correctly states that web service top-level `useFirewall` requires `application-load-balancer` and keeps that distinct from CDN firewall behavior.

The batch job spot guidance is mostly aligned with the source: `useSpotInstances` defaults to false, can save up to 90%, can be interrupted, sends `SIGTERM`, allows 120 seconds to shut down, and is best paired with `retryConfig`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft follows the required Stacktape config example format and has well-grounded coverage for web-service load balancing and batch-job Spot behavior. The main factual risk is that large parts of the page make Stacktape-specific claims about resources whose source files were not provided, plus a few specific defaults and limits that are not grounded in the provided source.

issues.statement: The compute comparison table says a Batch job has `Max run time` of `Unlimited`.
issues.reason: The provided BatchJob source exposes a `timeout` property described as the job's max run time. The draft may be trying to say there is no always-on server process, but `Unlimited` is too broad without a caveat that batch-job runtime can be capped with `timeout` and that the provided source does not document a fixed default maximum.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the table cell to something like `Configurable with timeout` or `Runs until completion unless timeout is set`.

evidence.quote: #### Max run time in seconds. The job is killed if it exceeds this, then retried if `retryConfig` is set.

issues.statement: The CDN section says `PriceClass_100` is the cheapest option, `PriceClass_All` is the default, and gives concrete CloudFront request and transfer pricing.
issues.reason: The provided WebService source only establishes that a web service can have a `cdn?: CdnConfiguration`. It does not include the `CdnConfiguration` definition, supported price class values, Stacktape's default price class, or the quoted CDN pricing. The AWS pricing model is acceptable background, but the Stacktape-specific default is not grounded in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the high-level recommendation to use a CDN for cacheable traffic, but remove or verify the `PriceClass_All` default and concrete pricing against the CDN configuration/pricing source before publishing.

evidence.quote: #### Put a CDN (CloudFront) in front of this service for caching and lower latency worldwide.

issues.statement: The log-retention table states default retention for Lambda functions, container workloads, and relational databases, alongside batch jobs.
issues.reason: The provided source only supports the batch-job logging default of 90 days. The other default-retention numbers may be correct elsewhere, but they are not grounded in the provided source files for this page.
issues.suggestedFix: Retain the batch-job default if useful, and remove the Lambda/container/database default rows unless the corresponding source files are available and confirm those defaults.

evidence.quote:
#### How many days to keep logs.
   * @default 90

issues.statement: The ARM/Graviton section claims `x86_64` is the default, `arm64` saves about 20% for Lambda and container workloads, Stacktape buildpack packaging builds for the selected architecture automatically, and container workloads set architecture under `resources`.
issues.reason: No LambdaFunction, container base type, or architecture-property source was provided. The web-service source in this prompt does not itself define `architecture`, and the batch-job source does not define it either.
issues.suggestedFix: Either provide the Lambda/container source files for verification or narrow the section to source-backed guidance. Avoid stating Stacktape defaults or build behavior unless the relevant types or validators confirm them.

issues.statement: The Lambda optimization section states Lambda memory-to-vCPU ratios, a default timeout of 10 seconds, provisioned-concurrency deployment delay, and cold-start ranges.
issues.reason: These claims are not grounded in the provided source files. Some are general AWS facts, but the `timeout` default and `provisionedConcurrency` behavior are Stacktape-specific enough to require Lambda source support for this page.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify against `LambdaFunctionProps` and related validators, or remove Stacktape-specific defaults and keep only general right-sizing advice.

issues.statement: The database sections claim Aurora Serverless v2 capacity ranges, RDS monthly examples, `multiAz` cost behavior, DynamoDB on-demand being the Stacktape default, Upstash no-idle-cost behavior, and Redis cluster starting price.
issues.reason: No relational database, DynamoDB, Redis, or Upstash source files were provided. These sections contain many concrete defaults, supported ranges, and prices that cannot be verified from the supplied source.
issues.suggestedFix: Add the relevant resource source files and pricing sources, or reduce the database content to cautious, non-numeric guidance with links to the dedicated resource pages.

issues.statement: The NAT/VPC section claims `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT: true` enables NAT Gateway for container workloads, container services have internet access by default without NAT, Lambda `joinDefaultVpc: true` removes direct internet access, and Stacktape auto-creates VPC endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB.
issues.reason: No Lambda networking or container networking source was provided. These are Stacktape-specific networking behaviors that would materially affect configuration choices.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify against the relevant Lambda and container workload source files before keeping the section. If those sources are unavailable, remove the Stacktape-specific NAT and VPC endpoint details.

issues.statement: The dev-mode and budget-alert sections describe local Docker defaults for relational databases and DynamoDB, `dev.remote: true`, budget alert scoping, and AWS Budgets pricing.
issues.reason: The provided source files do not include dev-mode, database dev configuration, Console budget behavior, or budget-pricing sources. These claims may be true, but they are not grounded in the supplied source for this verification pass.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify these claims against dev-mode, database, Console cost, and budget source files, or shorten the sections to source-backed links without specific defaults and prices.

positiveFindings: The draft does not use hand-authored YAML Stacktape configuration and uses `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab for Stacktape config examples.

The web-service load-balancing guidance is mostly grounded in the provided `web-services.d.ts`: `http-api-gateway` default pricing, ALB base cost and feature requirements, NLB protocol use, and the top-level firewall constraint match the source.

The batch-job Spot guidance is grounded in `batch-jobs.d.ts`: `useSpotInstances`, up-to-90% savings, interruption behavior with `SIGTERM` and 120 seconds, and retry configuration are all present in the source.
All checked internal markdown links point to routes present in the provided documentation navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the two provided source files for the areas it touches: web-service load-balancing variants are all named, and the spot batch-job example demonstrates the relevant `useSpotInstances` and `retryConfig` properties. I found one medium API-completeness gap that matters for a cost-optimization page.

issues.statement: The batch-job cost guidance covers spot instances and retries, but does not mention `timeout` as a cost-control guardrail for long-running jobs.
issues.reason: `timeout` is a BatchJobProps property that caps max runtime and kills jobs that exceed it. On a cost optimization page, this is a practical control for preventing runaway batch jobs from billing indefinitely or much longer than expected.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short sentence in the batch-job section recommending `timeout` for jobs with an expected upper runtime, especially when combined with `retryConfig`. For example: “Set `timeout` on batch jobs with a known upper bound so a stuck job is killed and retried instead of continuing to bill.”

evidence.quote: Max run time in seconds. The job is killed if it exceeds this, then retried if `retryConfig` is set.

positiveFindings: The web-service load-balancing section names all three source variants: `http-api-gateway`, `application-load-balancer`, and `network-load-balancer`.
The spot-instance batch-job example is demonstrative: it sets `useSpotInstances: true` and includes `retryConfig`, matching the section’s topic.
The page distinguishes viewing cost, reducing cost, and controlling cost near the top through dashboards, per-resource breakdown, and budget-alert links.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Excellent end-of-page checklist that gives a first-time user a concrete action plan — this is the single most useful section for the target audience and few doc pages include it.
Concrete numbers throughout: ~20% ARM savings, ~$18/month for ALB, ~$1/million for API Gateway requests. These specifics let the reader make real decisions rather than guessing.
FeatureComparisonTable for the compute model comparison is scan-friendly and clearly answers the 'which compute type do I pick?' question up front.
FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS-behavior, and comparison questions) and the answers are well-calibrated at 2-4 sentences each — extractable by AI engines.

Decision-support framing is consistent: every opt-in feature covers when to enable it, when the default is fine, a cost or tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. This is the guide-not-recipe standard done right.
Opening Info callout directs readers to measure before optimizing — correct framing that prevents premature optimization.

retryConfig defaults are accurately grounded in the BatchJob source file (@default 1 for attempts, @default 0 for retryIntervalSeconds, @default 1 for retryIntervalMultiplier) and the surrounding prose explains each default clearly.
No mandatory style violations: all Stacktape config examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>`, no YAML blocks, no StacktapeConfig type pattern, no multi-command bash blocks.
problems: Log retention defaults table claims Lambda function default is 180 days. The provided source files do not confirm this value — only the BatchJob source confirms 90 days (@default 90 on BatchJobLogging.retentionDays). The Lambda and container workload retention defaults are unverified from the provided sources and should be confirmed or removed.

The `attempts: 3` description says 'the job retries up to 3 times' which implies 4 total runs. The BatchJob source JSDoc says 'Max retry attempts' with @default 1, and the page's own 'default is 1 — no retries' interpretation implies attempts=N means N total runs (not N retries). The two descriptions contradict each other: '3 means retries up to 3 times' vs 'default 1 means no retries'. The correct reading is likely: attempts=1 means 1 total run (no retries), attempts=3 means 3 total runs (2 retries).

Several property names used in code examples (scaling.minInstances, scaling.maxInstances, scaling.scalingPolicy.keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder, resources.architecture for WebService) are not confirmed in the provided source files (only web-services.d.ts and batch-jobs.d.ts were provided). These may be grounded in other source files, but reviewers relying only on the inline source cannot verify them.

The 'Use dev mode during development' section references `dev.remote: true` as a property path without source-file grounding in the provided files. This claim is precise enough to be wrong if the actual property differs.

The page mentions 'Graviton processors often deliver better performance at the lower price point' — the ~20% cost figure is widely accepted, but the performance claim is a broad generalization that varies significantly by workload type and should be softened or removed.
optionalImprovements: Fix the retryConfig.attempts description to be internally consistent: if 'default is 1 — no retries' is correct, then 'attempts: 3 means the job retries up to 3 times' should read 'attempts: 3 means the job can run up to 3 times total (2 retries after the first failure)'.

Verify the Lambda log retention default (claimed 180 days) and container workload default (claimed 90 days) against the Lambda and container source files before publishing. If unverifiable, narrow the claim to what the source confirms: 'Batch jobs default to 90 days. Check the resource's logging property for its default.'

Verify `scaling.minInstances`, `scaling.maxInstances`, `scaling.scalingPolicy.keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder`, and `resources.architecture` for WebService against their respective source type files. If any name differs, the code examples will fail type-checking and confuse readers who try to copy them.

Soften 'Graviton processors often deliver better performance' to 'Graviton processors can deliver equivalent or better performance for most application workloads' — or drop the performance claim entirely and let the cost saving stand on its own, since that's verifiable.

Verify `dev.remote: true` as a valid property path, or rephrase to 'dev mode connects to your deployed cloud resources by default — consult the dev mode docs to configure local vs. remote database connections' to avoid committing to a specific property name.

Consider adding a one-sentence note about data transfer costs in the CDN section: CloudFront can also reduce origin egress costs since CloudFront-to-origin transfer is cheaper than direct internet egress from Lambda or Fargate. This is a meaningful cost lever the page currently skips.

The Fargate CPU values listed (0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16) are accurate AWS Fargate values but are framed as 'Stacktape supports' — link to the web-service or container resource page for the authoritative list rather than enumerating them inline, which creates a maintenance burden.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Billing separation (AWS account vs Stacktape subscription) is stated explicitly and early — matches the section instructions exactly.

Concrete numbers drawn from verified source: ~$1/million for HTTP API Gateway, ~$18/month for ALB, batch job 90-day log retention, retryConfig defaults (attempts=1, retryIntervalSeconds=0, retryIntervalMultiplier=1) — all confirmed against batch-jobs.d.ts.

Every opt-in feature (ARM, spot instances, CDN, ALB, NAT Gateway) includes a decision signal: when to enable, when the default is fine, and a cost or tradeoff statement. This is the right structure for a production guide.

The compute model comparison FeatureComparisonTable (pricing model, scale-to-zero, max run time, traffic pattern) is the single most useful element on the page — gives the reader the framework to choose before reading further.
The inline retryConfig explanation after the spot instances example is outstanding: each field, its default, and what that means in plain English. A production engineer would bookmark this.

FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific questions, AWS service mechanics (DynamoDB on-demand vs provisioned, Fargate scale-to-zero), and choosing/comparison questions (Aurora Serverless v2 vs RDS, CDN for APIs). Ten distinct, non-padded entries.
The end-of-page checklist is actionable and matches the page's content exactly — it can be pasted into a PR checklist template.
Code examples are realistic: they combine multiple configuration options (arm64 + right-sized cpu/memory + autoscaling in one block) rather than showing single-property snippets.
problems: Log retention defaults table claims Lambda functions default to 180 days, container workloads to 90 days, and relational databases to 90 days — none of these are grounded in the provided source files. Only the batch job 90-day default is confirmed by batch-jobs.d.ts (@default 90). If any of these defaults are wrong, a production engineer will set suboptimal retention values.

The claim 'when using EC2 instanceTypes, the architecture setting is ignored, so choose Graviton instance families (c7g, m7g, r7g) directly' is not verifiable from the provided web-services.d.ts source. The source file shows the loadBalancing union but does not expose an architecture property or instanceTypes on SimpleServiceContainer. This claim may be correct but it is ungrounded by the provided files.

The retryConfig.attempts JSDoc in batch-jobs.d.ts names the field 'Max retry attempts before the job is marked as failed' with @default 1 — 'retry attempts' implies 1 = 1 retry (2 total runs), but the page explains it as 'default is 1 — no retries' (1 total run). The page's interpretation aligns with AWS Batch semantics, but the contradiction with the JSDoc wording will confuse engineers who read the type definition alongside the docs.

The NAT Gateway section says 'only enable usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT when you have a concrete requirement for static outbound IPs' but does not explain what Stacktape does instead — i.e., whether containers in public subnets can still reach the internet and how. Engineers routinely get this wrong and the omission leaves a gap at a high-stakes decision point.
optionalImprovements: Verify log retention defaults for Lambda functions, container workloads (web-service/private-service/worker-service), and relational databases against their respective source type files before publishing. If the Lambda default is not 180 days, the table is actively misleading.

Add a clarifying sentence near the retryConfig.attempts field: 'attempts counts total run attempts, not additional retries — attempts: 1 (the default) means the job runs once and is not retried.' This resolves the JSDoc naming ambiguity before it bites a reader.

Either remove the 'architecture: arm64 is ignored for EC2 instanceTypes' claim or ground it in the source types (look for architecture in SimpleServiceContainer or equivalent EC2 container workload props). If it is accurate, add a note explaining which resource types support EC2 mode.

The NAT Gateway section should briefly state the default behavior — containers in public subnets can reach the internet without a NAT Gateway (Fargate ENI gets a public IP) — so engineers know what they are opting out of when skipping usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT.

In the right-size Lambda memory section, mention the 1,769 MB = 1 vCPU threshold. Engineers profiling Lambda functions use this as the primary decision point for memory increases, and its omission makes the profiling guidance feel incomplete.

The load balancing section's '~500k requests/day' crossover threshold between API Gateway and ALB is a useful rough number but is not source-grounded — consider marking it with 'roughly' and noting that the actual crossover depends on request size and ALB LCU consumption, so engineers should calculate for their own traffic profile.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clearly distinguishes AWS resource billing from Stacktape subscription cost.
The page is easy to scan: short H2s, practical subsections, a checklist, a comparison table, and a broad FAQ all support AI extraction.
Stacktape config examples use the required <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/> pattern with a visible TypeScript tab and no hand-authored YAML.
The load-balancing section is well aligned with the provided WebService source: it names all three loadBalancing variants and captures the sourced pricing and feature tradeoffs.

The batch-job spot section is practical and largely grounded in the BatchJob source, including up to 90% savings, interruption behavior, SIGTERM, 120-second shutdown, retryConfig, and timeout guidance.
problems: Large parts of the page are not grounded in the provided source files. The source list only includes web-services.d.ts and batch-jobs.d.ts, but the draft makes detailed claims about Lambda pricing, Lambda memory behavior, ARM/Graviton savings, Fargate CPU tiers, databases, DynamoDB, Upstash Redis, CDN pricing, NAT Gateways, dev mode, budgets, and Console dashboards.

Several concrete numeric claims are unsupported by the provided sources: 5–10x compute-model cost difference, Lambda 15-minute max runtime, ARM/Graviton ~20% savings, Fargate CPU values, utilization below 30%, 7–14 day and 30–60 day log-retention recommendations, budget thresholds at 80% and 100%, and CloudFront no-base-fee statements.

The log-retention table is only partially source-grounded. Batch jobs have default retention 90 days in the source, but Lambda, container workload, and relational database defaults are not present in the provided files.

The page presents broad cost guidance as authoritative even where the provided source is silent. That violates the hard requirement to trust source code first and avoid inventing unsupported features, defaults, or constraints.

The compute comparison table contains unsupported values for Lambda and container services, including scale-to-zero behavior, max runtime, and pricing model details not present in the supplied source files.

The ARM/Graviton section includes unsupported defaults and behavior, including x86_64 as default, Stacktape build behavior for selected architecture, custom-artifact requirements, and EC2 instance-family recommendations.

The FAQ is useful for search, but many answers restate unsupported Stacktape-specific claims. FAQ allowance for general AWS facts does not cover unsupported Stacktape behavior such as specific config properties or Stacktape Console capabilities when console-app sources are absent.

Some opt-in sections provide decision support but lack source grounding for the opt-in itself in this source set, especially CDN, NAT Gateway, dev mode local databases, budget alerts, and database engine selection.
mandatoryFixes: Remove, narrow, or source every technical claim not supported by the provided source files. With the current source set, the page can safely cover WebService load balancing and BatchJob spot/retry/timeout cost controls, but not detailed Lambda, database, CDN, Console, NAT, dev mode, or budget behavior.

Replace unsupported concrete numbers with sourced numbers only. Keep sourced values such as http-api-gateway ~$1/million requests, application-load-balancer ~$18/month plus usage, above ~500k requests/day, spot savings up to 90%, SIGTERM plus 120 seconds, retry defaults, and batch-job log retention default 90 days.
Revise the compute comparison table so every cell is grounded in the supplied sources, or remove rows/columns whose values require Lambda/container-workload sources not included here.

Delete or rewrite the log-retention table unless additional source files are provided for Lambda, container workloads, and relational databases. The current table overstates the available source evidence.

Do not describe Stacktape Console workflows or capabilities beyond broad links unless console-app source files are included. The current dashboard, metrics, per-resource cost, and budget-alert descriptions need console sources or narrower wording.
Recheck every <CodeBlock intellisense> after revising prose to ensure imports, class names, defineConfig usage, and focus markers remain valid.
optionalImprovements: Make the page scope explicit if only web-service and batch-job sources are available, for example by reframing it as cost tips for web services and batch jobs instead of a broad AWS cost optimization page.
Split the broad checklist into sourced and linked items, or remove checklist entries that depend on unavailable source files.

Keep the FAQ, but revise answers so Stacktape-specific statements are limited to sourced WebService and BatchJob behavior; general AWS cost questions can remain if clearly framed as AWS background rather than Stacktape behavior.
Add a small table for WebService loadBalancing variants with sourced pricing, constraints, and when-to-use guidance; that would be the strongest source-grounded section on the page.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the two provided source files for web-service load balancing and batch-job Spot/retry behavior, but it makes many Stacktape-specific cost and configuration claims that are not grounded in the provided sources. The highest-impact problems are unsupported architecture, Fargate scaling, logging-retention, CDN, database, and dev-mode claims; the source set only substantiates web-service load balancing and batch-job Spot/retry details.

issues.statement: “Switching from `x86_64` (the default) to `arm64` (AWS Graviton) saves ~20% on compute costs… Set `architecture: 'arm64'` on Lambda functions… For container-based resources … set `architecture: 'arm64'` in the `resources` block.”
issues.reason: The provided source files do not document any `architecture` property, any default architecture, or any Graviton/ARM pricing claim. The web-service source only shows `WebServiceProps extends SimpleServiceContainer`; the expanded `resources` shape is not present in the provided source. This would directly affect configuration choices.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove or narrow the ARM/Graviton section unless the page source list includes the Lambda and container resource types that define `architecture` and its defaults. If retained, ground it in the relevant `.d.ts` files and avoid claiming a default or percentage savings unless sourced.

issues.statement: “AWS Fargate requires a minimum of 1 running instance — it cannot scale to zero.” and the comparison table says “Container service” scale-to-zero is `false`.
issues.reason: The provided web-service source says a web service is a container running 24/7, but it does not document Fargate minimum instance behavior, scaling defaults, or private/worker service behavior. The claim is broader than the source and would affect whether readers choose Lambda or containers.
issues.suggestedFix: Limit the sourced claim to Stacktape web services being “a container running 24/7 with a public HTTPS URL.” Only discuss Fargate minimum instances or private/worker services if their source files are included and confirm the behavior.

evidence.quote: * #### A container running 24/7 with a public HTTPS URL.

issues.statement: “Right-size the `resources` block. Fargate bills for the CPU/memory combination you request… Start small (`cpu: 0.25`, `memory: 512`)…” and “Stacktape supports these Fargate vCPU values: `0.25`, `0.5`, `1`, `2`, `4`, `8`, and `16`.”
issues.reason: The provided source files do not define the web-service/container `resources` property or supported Fargate CPU/memory combinations. The only visible CPU/memory source is for `BatchJobResources`, where CPU is a generic number and memory is MB; it does not support the specific Fargate values claimed.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the specific Fargate CPU list and starter values unless the source list includes the container workload resource type that defines them. Keep only general right-sizing guidance or cite the correct container resource source.

issues.statement: “Each resource type in Stacktape has a default log retention period: Lambda function 180 days; Container workloads 90 days; Batch jobs 90 days; Relational databases 90 days.”
issues.reason: Only the batch-job default log retention is present in the provided sources. The Lambda, container workload, and relational database defaults are not grounded in the supplied source files. The section also generalizes “each resource type” beyond what is provided.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the sourced batch-job default of 90 days, or add the relevant Lambda/container/database source files before documenting their defaults. Avoid the “each resource type” framing unless all defaults are sourced.

issues.statement: “Set `retentionDays` in the `logging` block of each resource” followed by a LambdaFunction example using `logging.retentionDays`.
issues.reason: The provided source confirms `logging.retentionDays` only for `BatchJobLogging`. It does not include Lambda function types, so the Lambda-specific configuration example is not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the example to `BatchJob` if this page is limited to the provided sources, or add the Lambda source file that defines `LambdaFunctionProps.logging.retentionDays`.

issues.statement: “If you use `stacktape-lambda-buildpack` packaging, Stacktape builds for the selected architecture automatically. With `custom-artifact` packaging, you must pre-compile for the target architecture yourself.”
issues.reason: The provided sources do not include Lambda packaging types or behavior. This is a specific Stacktape build behavior claim and is not supported by the supplied files.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the packaging-build behavior claim or ground it in the Lambda/function packaging source files. Keep architecture compatibility discussion general unless the source documents build behavior.

issues.statement: “Enable a CDN on your web service when it returns cacheable content… CloudFront has no monthly base fee — you pay per-request plus per-GB data transfer.”
issues.reason: The web-service source only states that the `cdn` property puts CloudFront in front of the service for caching and lower latency. It does not document CloudFront pricing, cacheability guidance, price classes, or the statement that enabling CDN reduces Lambda/container/data transfer costs.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the source-grounded statement that `cdn` places CloudFront in front of a web service for caching and lower latency. Move pricing and price-class guidance to a CDN page or add CDN source/pricing files to this page’s source list.

issues.statement: “The `loadBalancing` property accepts three types” and the three variants are described, but the section does not show the required `ports` configuration for `network-load-balancer`.
issues.reason: The source explicitly says `network-load-balancer` requires explicit `ports` configuration and the `properties` field is required. The draft mentions this in prose but gives no example or concrete shape, while giving stronger decision support to the other load-balancing modes. This may leave readers without the required configuration for the NLB variant.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short TypeScript `CodeBlock` for `network-load-balancer` showing `loadBalancing: { type: 'network-load-balancer', properties: { ports: [...] } }`, or explicitly link readers to the NLB/web-service reference for the required shape.

positiveFindings: The batch-job Spot section is well grounded: the draft correctly states that `useSpotInstances` can save up to 90%, can be interrupted, sends `SIGTERM`, gives 120 seconds to shut down, and should be combined with `retryConfig` for restartable jobs.

The web-service load-balancing variants match the provided union: `http-api-gateway`, `application-load-balancer`, and `network-load-balancer`. The pricing and feature notes for API Gateway, ALB, and NLB largely track the JSDoc in `web-services.d.ts`.

The draft correctly distinguishes top-level `useFirewall` on a web service from CDN firewall attachment by linking the top-level firewall requirement to `application-load-balancer` rather than flattening it into a broad web-service constraint.
The `BatchJob` retry defaults in the draft match the source: `attempts` defaults to 1, `retryIntervalSeconds` defaults to 0, and `retryIntervalMultiplier` defaults to 1.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the two provided source files for web-service load balancing and batch-job Spot/retry behavior, but it makes many Stacktape-specific claims about resources whose source files were not provided. The highest-impact issues are unsupported broad claims about compute, architecture, databases, logging defaults, dev mode, NAT, and budgets that cannot be verified from the supplied sources.

issues.statement: "For container-based resources ([web services], [private services], [worker services]), set `architecture: 'arm64'` in the `resources` block. This applies to Fargate workloads — when using EC2 `instanceTypes`, the `architecture` setting is ignored..."
issues.reason: The provided web-service source does not define the nested `resources` shape, `architecture`, EC2 `instanceTypes`, or the behavior that architecture is ignored for EC2-backed workloads. This is Stacktape-specific configuration guidance that changes what readers configure, but it is not grounded in the supplied source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove or narrow this claim unless the relevant container workload type source is included. If retained, cite/source the type that defines `resources.architecture`, EC2 `instanceTypes`, and the ignore behavior.

issues.statement: "Stacktape supports these Fargate vCPU values: `0.25`, `0.5`, `1`, `2`, `4`, `8`, and `16`. Each CPU tier has an allowed memory range."
issues.reason: The supplied web-service source does not include the container resources type or any allowed Fargate CPU values. This numeric configuration claim is Stacktape-specific and would directly affect sizing decisions.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the exact CPU list unless the source file that defines container workload resource limits is included. A cautious alternative is to say that container CPU and memory are configured in the workload `resources` block and link to the relevant resource page.

issues.statement: "Each resource type in Stacktape has a default log retention period: Lambda function 180 days; Container workloads 90 days; Batch jobs 90 days; Relational databases 90 days."
issues.reason: Only the batch-job default retention is supported by the supplied sources. The Lambda, container workload, and relational database retention defaults are not present in the provided files, and the table presents them as sourced Stacktape defaults.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the batch-job default if useful, but remove the broader table unless the Lambda, container workload, and relational database logging type sources are provided. Alternatively, phrase only the sourced claim: batch-job logs default to 90 days.

issues.statement: "For relational databases and DynamoDB, dev mode can run a local Docker container. Set `dev.remote: true` only when you need to test against the actual deployed database..."
issues.reason: The provided sources contain no dev mode configuration, relational database type, DynamoDB type, or `dev.remote` property. This is Stacktape-specific behavior and configuration guidance that is unsupported by the supplied source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove this paragraph or replace it with a generic link to the dev mode page unless the relevant dev mode and resource type sources are supplied.

issues.statement: "In this example, `attempts: 3` means the job retries up to 3 times on failure or interruption..."
issues.reason: The source describes `attempts` as max retry attempts, but the wording in the draft says the job retries three times, which can be read as three retries after the initial run. The source wording is narrower and does not support the draft's expanded interpretation.
issues.suggestedFix: Write cautiously: "`attempts: 3` sets the maximum retry attempts to 3." Avoid explaining it as additional retries beyond the initial run unless runtime source confirms that behavior.

evidence.quote:
#### Max retry attempts before the job is marked as failed.
   * @default 1
   */
  attempts?: number;

issues.statement: "The `scaling.maxInstances` value caps how many instances Fargate can run simultaneously — this is your cost ceiling."
issues.reason: The provided web-service source does not define the `scaling` property or its semantics. The claim that `maxInstances` is a Fargate instance cap and cost ceiling is Stacktape-specific behavior not grounded in the supplied files.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the precise `scaling.maxInstances` explanation unless the container workload scaling type source is included. If source is available elsewhere, cite the exact scaling property and its documented behavior.

issues.statement: "Budgets can be scoped to your whole organization or to individual stacks."
issues.reason: The provided sources contain no budgets or Console cost-management source. This Stacktape-specific claim about budget scoping is unsupported by the supplied source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the sentence to "Set up budget alerts to catch spending spikes" and link to `/managing-costs/budgets`, or include the budget/Console source that proves supported scopes.

issues.statement: Claims about Lambda memory limits/runtime, provisioned concurrency, database engines, DynamoDB billing modes, Upstash Redis, NAT Gateway configuration, CDN price classes, and cost dashboard behavior are not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.reason: These topics may be correct in the broader codebase or AWS context, but the supplied source list only contains `web-services.d.ts` and `batch-jobs.d.ts`. The draft presents several Stacktape-specific properties and defaults outside those files as facts.
issues.suggestedFix: Either provide the relevant source files for those sections or remove/narrow the Stacktape-specific claims to links and general guidance that does not assert unsupported properties or defaults.

positiveFindings: The web-service load-balancing section matches the supplied `WebServiceProps.loadBalancing` variants and correctly distinguishes `http-api-gateway`, `application-load-balancer`, and `network-load-balancer`.
The draft correctly scopes top-level `useFirewall` to application load balancing and does not flatten it into the separate `cdn.useFirewall` path.

The batch-job Spot guidance is strongly grounded: `useSpotInstances` saves up to 90%, jobs can be interrupted, interrupted containers receive `SIGTERM`, and the source states a 120-second graceful shutdown window.
The Stacktape configuration examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and do not include hand-authored YAML or `StacktapeConfig` plain-object style.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly useful and several WebService and BatchJob claims are well aligned with the provided type sources, especially load balancing and BatchJob spot/retry behavior. The main audit risk is that several concrete Stacktape-specific cost claims are not grounded in the supplied source set, and one WebService union variant is named without its required configuration caveat.

issues.statement: “Switching from `x86_64` (the default) to `arm64` (AWS Graviton) saves ~20% on compute costs.”
issues.reason: The provided source files do not define an `architecture` property, its default, or any ARM/Graviton pricing discount. Because the page gives a concrete percentage and a default, this needs source support from the relevant Lambda/container type files or pricing source.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add the source files that define `architecture` defaults and pricing, or narrow the wording to avoid a precise default and percentage. For example: “Where supported by the resource, `arm64` can reduce AWS compute pricing compared with `x86_64`; verify native dependency compatibility before switching.”

issues.statement: “For container-based resources ... set `architecture: 'arm64'` in the `resources` block. This applies to Fargate workloads — when using EC2 `instanceTypes`, the `architecture` setting is ignored...”
issues.reason: The supplied WebService source does not expose the nested `resources` type, does not show an `architecture` field, and does not mention EC2 `instanceTypes` behavior. The BatchJob source has `instanceTypes` commented out, so this paragraph is not grounded in the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove or soften the EC2/architecture behavior unless the source list includes the container workload resources type that defines it. Keep the Lambda/container ARM examples only if their properties are visible in supplied source files.

issues.statement: “The `loadBalancing` property accepts three types... `network-load-balancer` is for non-HTTP traffic...”
issues.reason: The draft correctly names all three WebService load balancing union variants, but the Network Load Balancer variant requires explicit `ports` configuration. The page says NLB is available but omits that required property, which can mislead readers choosing that variant.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a sentence in the NLB paragraph: “`network-load-balancer` requires an explicit `ports` list; each entry defines the public port, optional protocol (`TCP` or `TLS`), and optional container port.”

issues.statement: “Stacktape supports these Fargate vCPU values: `0.25`, `0.5`, `1`, `2`, `4`, `8`, and `16`. Each CPU tier has an allowed memory range.”
issues.reason: The supplied source files do not include the container workload `resources` type behind `SimpleServiceContainer`, so the exact Fargate CPU values and memory constraints are not grounded in the provided source set.
issues.suggestedFix: Either include the source file that defines the container resource CPU/memory union, or replace the exact list with cautious guidance such as “Choose the smallest supported CPU and memory combination that keeps the service responsive; see the WebService API reference for allowed combinations.”

issues.statement: “Each resource type in Stacktape has a default log retention period: Lambda function 180 days; Container workloads 90 days; Batch jobs 90 days; Relational databases 90 days.”
issues.reason: Only the BatchJob logging default is present in the supplied sources. The Lambda, container workload, and relational database defaults are not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the BatchJob default if this source set remains unchanged, and avoid listing defaults for resource types whose logging types are not supplied. Alternatively include the relevant Lambda, container workload, and relational database type files.

issues.statement: “Container workloads in Stacktape can enable a NAT Gateway via `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT: true`.”
issues.reason: The supplied sources do not include `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT` on WebService or BatchJob. The source files shown for this page do not ground the property name or the stated static outbound IP use case.
issues.suggestedFix: Add the source file that defines `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT`, or remove the property-level claim and link to the relevant compute resource pages for VPC networking details.

issues.statement: “For relational databases and DynamoDB, dev mode can run a local Docker container. Set `dev.remote: true` only when you need to test against the actual deployed database...”
issues.reason: No dev-mode, relational database, or DynamoDB source files are included. The claim may be true elsewhere, but it is not grounded in the provided source files for this audit.
issues.suggestedFix: Either include the dev-mode and database source files or rewrite this section as a link-only recommendation: “Use dev mode during development; see the dev mode overview and local databases pages for which resources can run locally.”

positiveFindings: The load balancing section names all three WebService union variants: `http-api-gateway`, `application-load-balancer`, and `network-load-balancer`.
The BatchJob spot instance section is demonstrative: the code example sets `useSpotInstances`, `timeout`, and `retryConfig`, matching the section topic.
The draft correctly distinguishes top-level WebService `useFirewall` as an Application Load Balancer path and separately mentions CDN use without flattening those constraints.
The page clearly separates viewing costs through dashboards, controlling costs through budget alerts, and reducing costs through optimization recommendations.

pipelineReviewSummary: reviewer-avg 7.3, hard blockers 9, advisory high 0, verifier medium 13
